source_test: hand-specified (PairedDataShould.cs has no duplicate-value case); exercises .NET Array.BinarySearch's midpoint-driven match index on arrays with duplicate x (flat frequency) / duplicate y (flat damage) segments, which std::lower_bound (always the FIRST equal element) can disagree with. expected values are PINNED from the real C# via tools/oracle_emitter / tools/verify_oracles.py -- not hand-computed.

assertions.note: exact match inside a 3-wide duplicate x run -- Array.BinarySearch's midpoint lands on the MIDDLE duplicate (index 2, y=25), std::lower_bound would return the FIRST duplicate (index 1, y=20)
boundary sanity: first array element, both algorithms agree
boundary sanity: last array element, both algorithms agree
insertion-point sanity just above the duplicate run -- same insertion point either way
insertion-point sanity just below the duplicate run -- same insertion point either way
exact match on a duplicate run starting at index 0 -- Array.BinarySearch's midpoint lands on index 2 (y=15), std::lower_bound would return index 0 (y=5)
exact match on a non-duplicate value after the run -- both algorithms agree
below the curve -- clamps to first y
insertion point between the duplicate run and the next value -- same insertion point either way

exact match inside a 3-wide duplicate y run (flat damage segment) -- Array.BinarySearch's midpoint lands on the MIDDLE duplicate (index 2, x=3), std::lower_bound would return the FIRST duplicate (index 1, x=2)
boundary sanity: first array element, both algorithms agree
boundary sanity: last array element, both algorithms agree
insertion-point sanity just above the duplicate run -- same insertion point either way
insertion-point sanity just below the duplicate run -- same insertion point either way
